perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees to comply with all United States export controls with respect to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees to consider complying with United States export controls with respect to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees to comply' to 'agrees to consider complying' introduces ambiguity, making the obligation discretionary rather than mandatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR mandates strict adherence to export control regulations. By suggesting that compliance is merely a consideration, the modified text contradicts the mandatory nature of these regulations, creating a risk of non-compliance and potential violations of U.S. export law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: EAR | Bureau of Industry and Security An official website of the United States government Here's how you know Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'agrees to comply' with 'agrees to consider complying,' making compliance discretionary instead of mandatory. The Law Explanation states that the EAR mandates strict adherence to export control regulations, and suggesting compliance is merely a consideration creates a risk of non-compliance. Scraped Law Snippet 2 highlights that the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) exist under 15 CFR 730-774. While neither snippet explicitly states 'compliance must be mandatory,' the overall implication from the Law Explanation and Snippet 2 is that export controls are not optional. The change fundamentally weakens the obligation to comply, potentially leading to violations of export law as described in the Law Explanation. This falls under the Ambiguity Legal Contradiction definition because the ambiguity introduced makes the legal obligation difficult to enforce, potentially resulting in non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information remains the property of the Company and no license or other rights in the Confidential Informati on is granted hereby.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information generally remains the property of the Company, and licensing shall be determined on a case-by-case basis.
perturbation.explanation: Introducing "generally" and "case-by-case basis" creates ambiguity about the ownership and licensing of the Confidential Information. This conflicts with the clarity required in intellectual property law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CUTSA protects trade secrets, and requires clear and unambiguous designation of ownership. The modified text's vagueness about property rights and licensing undermines the protection afforded by CUTSA by clouding the ownership and usage rights of the confidential information, making enforcement more difficult.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding ownership and licensing of confidential information by adding "generally" and "case-by-case basis." This directly contradicts the need for clear designation of ownership as highlighted in the Law Explanation, which states CUTSA requires clear and unambiguous designation of ownership. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) under California Civil Code Section 3426, supports the importance of trade secret protection, which is undermined by the created ambiguity. The change weakens the legal protection of confidential information, making it more difficult to enforce, thus establishing a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees to maintain the confidence of the Confidential Information and to prevent its unauthorized dissemination
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees to endeavor to maintain the confidence of the Confidential Information and to discourage its unauthorized dissemination
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'maintain the confidence' to 'endeavor to maintain the confidence' and 'prevent its unauthorized dissemination' to 'discourage its unauthorized dissemination' weakens the legal obligation from a strict requirement to a weaker, effort-based one.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1644 implies a stricter interpretation of contract language when safeguarding confidential information is paramount. By replacing 'maintain' with 'endeavor to maintain' and 'prevent' with 'discourage', the obligation becomes less stringent, potentially failing to meet the implied duty of care under California law for protecting sensitive data.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1644. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1644. The words of a contract are to be understood in their ordinary and popular sense, rather than according to their strict legal meaning; unless used by the parties in a technical sense, or unless a special meaning is given to them by usage, in which case the latter must be followed. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The change from 'maintain' and 'prevent' to 'endeavor to maintain' and 'discourage' introduces ambiguity, weakening the obligation to protect confidential information. The original text creates a strict obligation. The altered text weakens it. The Law Explanation states that California Civil Code § 1644 implies a stricter interpretation of contract language when safeguarding confidential information is paramount. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'The words of a contract are to be understood in their ordinary and popular sense'. Here, the ordinary sense of 'maintain' and 'prevent' imply a stronger obligation than 'endeavor to maintain' and 'discourage'. This weakening could potentially fail to meet the implied duty of care under California law for protecting sensitive data, creating a legal contradiction because an ambiguous legal obligation now exists.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees to return to the Company immediately upon the Company’s written request all Confidential Information, including but not limited to all computer programs, documentation, notes, plans, drawings, and copies thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees to, where reasonably feasible, return to the Company upon request Confidential Information, including, where practical, computer programs, documentation, notes, plans, drawings, and copies thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Adding qualifiers like 'where reasonably feasible' and 'where practical' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to return confidential information. This makes the return of the information conditional rather than mandatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1856 prioritizes the written agreement as the definitive record of the parties' intent. Introducing subjective conditions such as 'where reasonably feasible' and 'where practical' allows for potential deviations from the clear mandate to return confidential information, thereby creating ambiguity in the interpretation and enforcement of the contractual obligation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1856 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 1. OF THE GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EVIDENCE [1855 - 1866] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 1856. (a) Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement. (b) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (c) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing or usage of trade or by course of performance. (d) The court shall determine whether the writing is intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein and whether the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (e) Where a mistake or imperfection of the writing is put in issue by the pleadings, this section does not exclude evidence relevant to that issue. (f
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by adding qualifiers like 'where reasonably feasible' and 'where practical,' weakening the obligation to return confidential information. This contradicts the principle that a written agreement should be the definitive record of the parties' intent, as stated in California Code of Civil Procedure § 1856. The added conditions create uncertainty in the interpretation and enforcement of the obligation, allowing potential deviations from the original mandate.
